coredata.dc:title: B, S, N co-doped carbon dots-based highly sensitive and rapid nanoprobe for monitoring of pepsin activity in human saliva and its cell imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Al-mashriqi, Haitham Saad
Sanga, Pascaline
Qaed, Eskandar
Chen, Jia
Li, Xin
Zhang, Yunyun
Qiu, Hongdeng
coredata.dc:description: Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) is widespread, but screening is difficult due to its non-specific symptoms. Detecting pepsin in saliva is a non-invasive and specific diagnostic method, yet it requires enhancements in reaction time, selectivity, and sensitivity. In this study, a highly sensitive detection method has been developed for monitoring pepsin activity in human saliva based on boron, sulfur, and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (BSN-CDs) for the first time. The manufactured BSN-CDs exhibit exceptional photoluminescent properties, high water solubility, and stability in varying salt concentrations and pH levels. The nanoprobe shows a fast response time and a wide detection range from 0 to 60 µg/mL for pepsin with a detection limit of 0.24 µg/mL, making it a valuable tool for clinical diagnostics. To our knowledge, this is the most responsive material for pepsin activity reported to date. The method’s viability was confirmed by accurately measuring pepsin in human saliva samples, achieving recovery rates between 96.0 % to 108.5 %. Moreover, the study investigates using these carbon dots for imaging HepG2 and SiHa cells, highlighting their broader biomedical potential. It concludes that BSN-CDs are promising nanomaterials for sensitive biomolecule detection and advanced imaging techniques.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Nanoprobe
Monitoring
Pepsin
Human saliva
Cell imaging

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was supported by the Light of West China\u201C Program from the Chinese Academy of Science (xbzg-zdsys-202008), National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 22374159 ), Youth Innovation Promotion Association CAS ( 2021420 ) and ANSO Scholarship for Young Talents in China, for the Chinese Academy of Sciences , and \u201DSponsored by UCAS Scholarship for International Students\u201C.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The schematic of the BSN-CDs synthesis method. (b, c, and d) Characteristic TEM images of BSN-CDs with 50, 20, and 10 nm diameter. (e) The size distribution histogram of BSN-CDs.

Characterizations of BSN-CDs. (a) FTIR spectrum, (b) XRD spectrum of BSN-CDs, (c) full-scanning XPS analysis of BSN-CDs, (d) Atomic percentage of elements, and the detailed spectra for C 1 s (e), N 1 s (f), O 1 s (g), B 1 s(h) and S 2P (i) bands.

(a) BSN-CDs properties, absorbance, excitation, and emission. (b) CIE graph of BSN-CDs. (c)The FL signals of BSN-CDs with diverse excitation wavelengths change. (d) The normalized FL spectra of BSN-CDs.

(a) FL intensity of BSN-CDs afterward the addition of diverse concertation of pepsin (0, 1, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45, 50, 60, 70, 80, 100, 120, and 140 µg/mL). (b) The relation curve between (F-F0/F0) of BSN-CDs and pepsin concentration. (c) A diagram depicting a potential mechanism for fluorescence pepsin detection based on BSN-CDs.

(a)The FL signals of BSN-CDs (red) BSN-CDs after adding pepsin (blue). (b) The UV–vis of BSN-CDs, BSN-CDs with pepsin, and pepsin. (c) The steady-state fluorescence lifetimes of BSN-CDs and BSN-CDs in the presence of pepsin. (d) The zeta potential graph compares BSN-CDs and BSN-CDs with pepsin.

(a)The selectivity of pepsin based on BSN-CDs in the existence of 120 µM of metal ions, 270 µM amino acids, and 400 µg/mL of enzymes. (c) The corresponding FL signals. (b) An interference study assessed pepsin activity with consistent circumstances and concentrations. The FL emissions of all the solutions were determined with excitation at 510 nm and emission at 650 nm. (d) The corresponding FL signals.

Fluorescence microscopy shots of HepG2 cells incubated with (200 μg ml−1 and 300 μg ml−1) at 37 °C, respectively. The corresponding images include those of BSN-CDs, nucleus tracker, bright-field images, and merged images. The scale bar is 10 µm.

Fluorescence microscopy shots of SiHa cells incubated with (30 μg ml−1 and 50 μg ml−1) at 37 °C, respectively. The corresponding images include those of BSN-CDs, nucleus tracker, bright-field images, and merged images. The scale bar is 10 and 20 µm.
Representation of pepsin detection activity in creating BSN-CDs and its application in SiHa and HepG2 cells.
Analytical performance of some reported fluorescent assays for pepsin detection.

head.ce:title: B, S, N co-doped carbon dots-based highly sensitive and rapid nanoprobe for monitoring of pepsin activity in human saliva and its cell imaging

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) is widespread, but screening is difficult due to its non-specific symptoms. Detecting pepsin in saliva is a non-invasive and specific diagnostic method, yet it requires enhancements in reaction time, selectivity, and sensitivity. In this study, a highly sensitive detection method has been developed for monitoring pepsin activity in human saliva based on boron, sulfur, and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (BSN-CDs) for the first time. The manufactured BSN-CDs exhibit exceptional photoluminescent properties, high water solubility, and stability in varying salt concentrations and pH levels. The nanoprobe shows a fast response time and a wide detection range from 0 to 60 µg/mL for pepsin with a detection limit of 0.24 µg/mL, making it a valuable tool for clinical diagnostics. To our knowledge, this is the most responsive material for pepsin activity reported to date. The method’s viability was confirmed by accurately measuring pepsin in human saliva samples, achieving recovery rates between 96.0 % to 108.5 %. Moreover, the study investigates using these carbon dots for imaging HepG2 and SiHa cells, highlighting their broader biomedical potential. It concludes that BSN-CDs are promising nanomaterials for sensitive biomolecule detection and advanced imaging techniques.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Pepsin, classified as an aspartic acid protease, is among the three key enzymes involved in protein digestion within the digestive system. It is commonly utilized for diagnosing neurological disorders and gastric hypoacidity. Additionally, pepsin plays a significant role in the food industry, particularly in the production of soy products and beer, due to its exceptional efficiency in cleaving peptide bonds between hydrophobic and aromatic amino acids [1–3]. A deficiency in pepsin can lead to gastric mucosal diseases and various digestive issues, including abdominal pain, bloating, vomiting, nausea, and acid reflux [4]. Pepsin-related gastroesophageal reflux disease(GERD) can damage the hypopharynx, esophagus, upper respiratory tract, and oropharynx [5]. Pepsin functions optimally in acidic environments and facilitates the digestion of proteins in food. However, an excess of pepsin can result in GERD. Research indicates that pepsin can lead to cell damage, inflammation, and the growth of tumors [6,7]. Pepsin in saliva is a straightforward, convenient, and non-invasive method for diagnosing the proximal reflux of gastric contents or laryngopharyngeal reflux (LPR) [8].

Consequently, there is a need for highly sensitive methods to detect pepsin in the food industry and for disease diagnosis. To date, numerous methodologies have been employed for the detection of pepsin, including enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays [9], electrochemical immunosensors [10], molecularly imprinted analysis [11], chromatography [12], capillary electrophoresis methods [13]. Despite their utility, these approaches are limited by their inability to perform rapid analysis and lack of selectivity. Furthermore, these methods require considerable time, complex procedures, and their sensitivity is often insufficient. Consequently, a significant need remains to develop straightforward and efficient methods for direct pepsin assays that offer high sensitivity and selectivity. Fluorometric methods are largely preferred due to their reliable sensing capabilities, accessibility, and low cost [14]. Lately, strategies have emerged for detecting pepsin that involve the use of pepsin digestion to induce fluorescence quenching in protein-coated fluorescent dyes or nanomaterials [15,16]. Nevertheless, the synthesis processes for these commonly used probes are complex, and the method's sensitivity and responsiveness to analytes could be further improved. Thus, designing well-crafted nanosensors for pepsin detection with low toxicity and rapid speed remains essential to enhance sensing performance and selectivity.

Recent advances in nanotechnology have introduced carbon dots (CDs), an innovative kind of fluorescent tiny particles with remarkable properties, making them promising tools in biosensing [17,18]. Carbon dots are characterized by their ease of synthesis, excellent biocompatibility, robust chemical inertness, and strong photoluminescent properties [19,20]. These features make CDs an attractive platform for developing optical biosensors [21]. In recent years, doped carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as a significant area of carbon-based nanomaterials. Doping with heteroatoms effectively tailors surface defects adjusts electronic energy values, and modifies the optical characteristics of CDs [22]. Boron (B), sulfur (S), and nitrogen (N) are the most commonly used doping atoms for carbon dots (CDs). These atoms, which have similar atomic radii to carbon and are located nearby in the periodic table, enhance the fluorescence characteristics of CDs and introduce functional groups for target monitoring [23,24]. Gong and co-workers described a straightforward one-step solvothermal method for producing full-color-emitting nitrogen/sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots [25]. Red emissive CDs doped with specific heteroatoms demonstrate significant potential for applications in critical bio-related fields [26,27]. Sun et al. devised a simple method to manufacture S, N co-doped CDs with red emissive for Fe3+ detection and theranostics [28]. Zhao et al. described a new fluorescent investigation based on N-doped carbon dots with red emissive to detect copper ions [29]. In other studies, researchers have synthesized various types of red-emissive carbon dots, including N, P-doped CDs, and utilized them as platforms for the detection of antibiotics and anticancer drugs, thereby enhancing their suitability for bioanalysis [30–32]. These findings underscore the significant academic potential of doped carbon dots (d-CDs) emitting red light for various biological applications, including sensitive detection, precise target labeling, and high-resolution cell imaging. Incorporating carbon dots into biosensing for enzyme detection introduces a novel approach that combines simplicity, sensitivity, and rapid response times. This integration also holds promise for miniaturization and real-time analysis in biosensing applications [33,34].

Taking this into consideration, for the first time, we have designed an innovative fluorescent probe to detect pepsin activity, employing BSN-CDs synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal process with 2,5-Diaminobenzenesulfonic acid and 4-aminophenylboronic acid hydrochloride as starting materials for their ability to facilitate key functional group formation, enabling efficient coupling reactions and enhancing material properties for targeted applications. These BSN-CDs exhibit red fluorescence (FL) emission at 600 nm upon excitation at 510 nm. This innovation boasts significant advantages, including a straightforward preparation procedure, cost-effective source materials, excellent photostability, and superior water solubility. The co-doping with B, S, and N atoms endows the BSN-CDs with outstanding optical characteristics, minimal cytotoxicity, and good biocompatibility on human cells. Notably, no additional surface modification was required to introduce binding sites. Introducing pepsin resulted in a significant enhancement of the BSN-CDs FL intensity, showcasing the sensitivity and selectivity of our technique. To our knowledge, this is the first study to use low-cost and red emissive carbon dots with exceptional sensitivity and specificity for evaluating pepsin activity in human saliva accurately. Furthermore, the cell imaging examination indicated that the BSN-CDs solution did not alter the morphology of HepG2 and SiHa cells, highlighting its effective cellular imaging capabilities and excellent biocompatibility., which is considered a promising candidate for clinical diagnosis (Scheme 1 ).

The BSN-CDs were manufactured through hydrothermal handling, utilizing temperature control based on this study with slight adjustments [35]. Specifically, a solution comprising 4-aminophenylboronic acid hydrochloride (0.085 g) and 2,5-Diaminobenzenesulfonic acid (0.085 g) dissolved in 25 mL of deionized water. After subjecting the solution to 3 min of sonication, it was moved into a Teflon autoclave. Following heating at 180 °C for 8 h, the autoclave was allowed to naturally cool to room temperature. Then, the prepared reaction underwent filtration using a 0.22 μm filter membrane and subsequent centrifugation at 11000 rpm for 20 min to eliminate larger particulates. The organized solution gotten was preserved for subsequent dialysis (1000 Da) throughout 16 h. The concentrated BSN-CDs solution was stored at 4 °C for future use. Furthermore, the purified BSN-CDs solution underwent freeze-drying to yield a powdered form of BSN-CDs, subsequently pulverized for FT-IR, XRD, and XPS analyses.

The detection of pepsin was assessed through the following experimental procedure: A pepsin stock solution of 1 mg/mL was prepared to examine the enhanced responsiveness of pepsin to the emitting fluorescence by BSN-CDs. Preparation of pepsin solutions spanning concentrations from 0 to 140 µg/mL were obtained by diluting the stock solution with Gly-HCl buffer (50 mM, pH 2.5). In brief, 5 µL of BSN-CDs was added to varying concentrations of pepsin, and the solution was then combined with Gly-HCl buffer to reach a total volume of 1.5 mL. The fluorescence intensity was recorded afterward incubating the tube for 1.5 min at room temperature and with excitation at 510 nm. The specificity of the probe was assessed using a similar methodology as described above.

Initially, saliva samples (1–3) were collected from human volunteers from Gansu Provincial Hospital in Lanzhou, China, and analyzed for varying pepsin concentrations. Given that numerous components in saliva can interfere with the analyte of interest, the samples were diluted 100-fold with a citric acid solution, subsequently frozen, and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min. Finally, the samples were sifted using a 0.22 µm pore membrane before analysis [36,37].

HepG2 and SiHa cells in the logarithmic progress phase were used in this study. The cell suspension was changed to a concentration of approximately 5x104 cellsmL−1, and 200 μL of this cell suspension was added for every well on a 96-well culture medium. The culture were reserved at 37 °C with 5 % CO2 for about 24 h until a monolayer of cells was established. Following this, a series of BSN-CDs solutions with concentrations of 10, 20, 50, 100, 200, and 500 µg/mL, prepared by diluted with PBS, were incorporated into the wells. Each concentration was verified in six replicate wells. After a 24-h incubation time, 20 μL of MTT solution (5 mg/mL) was put into every well, and the plates were left to incubate for a further 4 h under the same circumstances. After that, the culture was stopped, the supernatant was thrown away, and 200 μL of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was injected into each well. The cultures medium were set on a shaker and oscillated at a small speed for 10 min to guarantee that the produced crystals were completely dissolved. Using a microplate reader, the absorbance (OD) of separately well was determined at 492 nm. The experiment also contained a conventional cell control.

The synthesis process of BSN-CDs is depicted in Fig. 1 a, utilizing 3-amino benzene boronic acid and 2,5-diamino benzene sulfonic acid as sources. The resulting products' morphology and diameter size were investigated using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As depicted in (Fig. 1b, c, and d), the BSN-CDs are nearly spherical and demonstrate high monodispersity in water. Upon conducting statistical analysis of over one hundred particles, it was determined that the BSN-CDs possess a size distribution ranging from 0.5 nm to 3 nm, with a medium size of 1.72 nm (Fig. 1e). As demonstrated in the inset of Fig. 1c, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was employed to describe the crystal structure of BSN-CDs, displayed the (100)-crystal plane of graphite coordinated by crystal stripes of carbon material with spaces of 0.21 nm.

Analyses using FT-IR was carried out to determine the functional groups and structures of BSN-CDs. As illustrated in Fig. 2 a, N–H, and O–H bonds are evidenced by the peaks observed at 3213.84–3500 cm−1 [38]. Additionally, the peak at 2962.2 and 2604.43 cm−1 corresponds to C-H and −SH vibrations, indicating the incorporation of sulfur within the BSN-CDs [39]. Furthermore, the peaks at 1723.59, 1497.96, and 1405.87 cm−1 confirm the existence of CO, CC, and C–N bonds, respectively [27,28]. The peak observed at 1254.9 cm−1 corresponds to the bending vibrations of the C–C and C–S bonds [23,40]. The prominent absorption patterns at 1198.1, 1104.1, and 1020 cm−1 are associated with the B–O–H stretching waves, the C–B bending feature, and the B–O–H bending vibration, respectively [27,41]. These absorption peaks collectively specify the effective incorporation of B, S, and N particles into the synthesized BSN-CDs.

XRD and XPS spectra were analyzed to obtain detailed information on the valence states, elemental contents, and surface composition of BSN-CDs. As depicted in Fig. 2b, the XRD pattern exhibits a broad peak centered around 24.5°, which corroborates the presence of small crystalline cores surrounded by a disordered surface structure in the BSN-CDs [42].

The full-range XPS spectrum (Fig. 2c) distinctly displayed five peaks at 167, 191.2, 284.3, 401, and 531 eV, belonged to S 2p, B 1s, C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively. As revealed in Fig. 2d, the spectrum analysis revealed the elemental conformation of BSN-CDs, identifying the existence of S, B, C, N, and O. According to the findings, these elements atomic percentages were 5 %, 0.6 %, 54.5 %,7.6 %, and 32.3 %, respectively. As illustrated in Fig. 2e, the high-resolution C 1s spectrum reveals the existence of C–B at 283.5 V, CC /C–C at 284.7 eV, C–O/C–S at 285.8 eV, respectively [43,44]. Additionally, the N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2f) displayed two prominent peaks belonged to pyridinic-type N atoms at 398.5 eV and pyrrolic-type N atoms at 400.5 eV, confirming the successful incorporation of nitrogen particles into the BSN-CDs [45]. The high-resolution O 1 s band (Fig. 2g) displays three distinct peaks at 532.02 eV, 531.14 eV, and 530.59 eV, ascribed to OC–O, C–O-C, and CO, respectively [46]. The B 1 s peak of BSN-CDs splitting into three different peaks at 186.50 eV, 188.50.4 eV, and 191.55 eV (Fig. 2h), indicating the presence of boron atoms in BSN-CDs in three different chemical states. This suggests that boron atoms are integrated into the CDs structure by forming B-sub-C bonds, participating in the reformation of the hexatomic ring, and forming various boron–oxygen–carbon compounds such as BC2O and BCO2 through reactions with epoxy and carbonyl groups [47,48]. The S 2p XPS range (Fig. 2i) exhibits three distinct peaks placed at 165.4 eV, 166.4 eV, and 168.0 eV, ascribed to −C-SOx- species, where x equals 2, 3, and 4, respectively [35,49]. The elements on the surface of BSN-CDs, as identified by XPS, aligned with the FT-IR findings. Both analyses verified that boron, sulfur, and nitrogen were co-doped into BSN-CDs, with functional groups such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amino groups on their surface.

The optical properties of the prepared BSN-CDs materials displayed in Fig. 3 , the UV/vis spectra reveal two distinct peaks of absorption at 234 and 282 nm, corresponding to the π–π* transition of the aromatic sp2 areas, and n-π* transition of the CO bond, respectively as depicted in Fig. 3a [50]. Additionally, the BSN-CDs exhibit maximum excitation and emission peaks at 510 nm and 600 nm, respectively. Upon irradiation with a hand-held UV lamp emitting light at 365 nm, the aqueous solution of BSN-CDs, tinged light red, produces a strong red fluorescence, as illustrated in the inset of Fig. 3a. The chromaticity color coordinates of BSN-CDs stand at (0.559, 0.439), further corroborating the manifestation of red fluorescence by BSN-CDs when subjected to UV radiation, as depicted in Fig. 3b. In contrast to the majority of documented CDs, the BSN-CDs exhibit a characteristic of excitation wavelength independence across a series of excitation wavelengths. As illustrated in Fig. 3c, the emission wavelength of BSN-CDs remains unaffected as the excitation wavelength varies from 400 nm to 510 nm. This observation suggests that incorporating nitrogen, sulfur, and boron atoms may play a role in modulating the surface states and sizes of CDs. Consequently, this leads to a relatively uniform distribution of sizes and surface state of BSN-CDs [35]. In the normalized fluorescence spectra depicted in Fig. 3d, the emission wavelength of BSN-CDs demonstrates only a minor redshift of approximately 3 nm. These findings indicate that the emission wavelength of BSN-CDs remains largely unaffected by changes in the excitation wavelength.

The chemical constancy of the organized nanosensor was assessed to widen the scope of practical applications for CDs in research. As shown in Fig. S1a, the BSN-CDs fluorescence (FL) signal remained stable even at a high NaCl concentration of 100 mM. This observation confirms that the highly concentrated ionic medium did not affect the FL indicator of BSN-CDs. Furthermore, the FL signal of BSN-CDs in 50 mM Gly-HCl at different pH levels increased as the pH range increased from 1.5 to 4.5, followed by a decrease (Fig. S1b). This behavior can be attributed to the protonation-deprotonation characteristics of the surface of BSN-CDs [51]. The FL intensity of BSN-CDs was measured for 90 min of light exposure via fluorescence light. As shown in Fig. S1c, there was no change in FL intensity, indicating that the synthesized BSN-CDs are highly stable during storage. Finally, as illustrated in Fig. S1d, the tolerability of BSN-CDs to temperature changes. The FL signal did not alteration as the temperature raised from 25 °C to 50 °C. This excellent stability makes the as-prepared BSN-CDs suitable for various practical applications.

The fluorescence of carbon dots is well recognized to stem from the recombination of electron-hole pairs through radiation following band gap excitation, and this fluorescence is sensitive to the surface states of the CDs [56–58]. Based on this principle, numerous mechanisms have been projected to design approaches for detecting various compounds, such as biomolecules [59,60], inorganic ions [61–63], and pharmaceutical composites [64,65], by leveraging the surface state fluorescence regulation of QDs. In this context, we propose employing the same approach for the turn-on sensing of pepsin using BSN-CDs. Kolekar and co-authors [57] discovered that adding d-penicillamine enhances the fluorescence signals of MPA-CdS QDs. They attributed this increase to the trap state surface passivation and activation by the inorganic shell, resulting from the incarceration of charges within the nanocrystalline MPA-CdS QDs. Lou and co-authors [66] proposed that creating an analyte ligand multifaceted on the surface of QDs limits ligand rotation and promotes an unchanging arrangement. This organized orientation and enhanced conformational stiffness of surface substituents can effectively safeguard the core, reduce quenching pathways to the medium, and boost fluorescence.

The attached analyses on the QD surface can also address surface imperfections and dangling bonds, thereby reducing non-radiative electron-hole recombination centers and enhancing fluorescence. Based on prior reports, numerous experiments were carried out on BSN-CDs before and after adding pepsin to examine the binding capability of pepsin to the surface of BSN-CDs. This interaction was established through experiments, including fluorescent, absorption, lifetime steady state, and zeta potential measurements of BSN-CDs before and after their interaction with pepsin. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the fluorescence spectra of BSN-CDs show a progressive increase in fluorescence intensity with the addition of pepsin. This enhancement is accompanied by a redshift, which can be attributed to removing local surface trap states by the added pepsin. This process increases the band gap of the BSN-CDs, leading to the observed fluorescence enhancement (Fig. 4c). As revealed in Fig. 5b, the free absorbance band spectrum of BSN-CDs and BSN-CDs with pepsin was analyzed. Upon the introduction of pepsin, an alteration in the absorbance band was noticed, which disappeared indicating an interaction between BSN-CDs and pepsin. This suggests that the presence of pepsin affects the optical properties of BSN-CDs, potentially through a conformational change or interaction that alters the electronic structure of the BSN-CDs. The lifetime of BSN-CDs was evaluated. However, after pepsin was introduced, a notable rise in the lifetime of BSN-CDs was noted (Fig. 5c and Table S1). This indicates that pepsin reduces the non-radiative decay of BSN-CDs while enhancing radiative decay. Consequently, the fluorescence source of BSN-CDs becomes high, which greatly boosts the fluorescence intensity [67]. The surface trap states passivation in BSN-CDs occurs through the binding of the O-H group of pepsin with the NH group of BSN-CDs. This interaction leads to the formation of different radiative electron-hole recombination centers [68,69]. Zeta potential calculations indicated that the first zeta potential of the BSN-CDs was −1.10 mV. However, after introducing the pepsin solution, the zeta potential significantly increased to −5.56 mV (Fig. 5d). This increase is likely due to the surface adsorption of pepsin by bonding among pepsin and the functional group of BSN-CDs. This exchange increases the negative charge on the surface of the CDs owing to the free carboxylate groups of pepsin. These observations propose that the adsorption of pepsin on the surface of BSN-CDs enhances the fluorescence intensity, accompanied by a slight red shift in the emission wavelength [57].

To validate the specificity of the detection method, various interfering substances, including metal ions, amino acids, and enzymes such as Ni2+, Cu2+, Na+, Ca2+, K+, Cl-, HCO3 –, Urea, Uric acid, Cys, Mal, Amy, Try, Ure, Cat, His, Ala, Ser, Asp, and Leu, were examined using identical sensing parameters. As shown in Fig. 6 a and c, all these potential interfering substances had a negligible effect on the detection process. The system's anti-interference capability was assessed by determining the FL signals of the produced BSN-CDs in the existence of pepsin mixed with various interfering agents. As shown in Fig. 6b and d, the FL intensity remained unchanged compared to when only pepsin was present in the sensing system. This demonstrates that the system exhibits robust tolerance and excellent selectivity for pepsin detection.

To further evaluate the accuracy and feasibility of the developed method based on BSN-CDs for detecting pepsin in human saliva, owing to its notable sensitivity and selectivity. As exposed in Table S2, the analysis discovered recoveries for human saliva extending from 96.0 % to 108.5 %. Additionally, the relative standard deviation (RSD) was below 3.92 %, which is satisfactory for measurable investigation of biological samples. These results indicate that the proposed analytical procedures are free from interfering substances when examining pepsin in real samples. Consequently, the technique can successfully and accurately measure pepsin activity in real-life samples.

Toxicology is an important concern for potential biological applications. To examine the cytotoxicity of BSN-CDs on HepG2 and SiHa cells, MTT assays were performed. The BSN-CDs demonstrated consistent biocompatibility as expected. Fig. S3a and b show that treatment with a BSN-CDs solution (500 and 50 μg/mL) caused moderate damage, with approximately 77.7 % of HePG2 cells and 72.3 % of SiHa cells remaining viable. Furthermore, as depicted in Fig. 7 , afterward 4 h of incubation with BSN-CDs at concentrations of (200 and 300 μg/mL) for HepG2 cells, and likewise in Fig. 8 , through 4 h of incubation with BSN-CDs (30 and 50 μg/mL) for SiHa cells at 37 °C with 5 % CO2, both HepG2 and SiHa cells exhibited strong red fluorescence signals within the cytoplasm, along with blue-stained nucleus localization via 4,6-dimidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) dye. This enabled us to examine the colocalization of these signals within both HepG2 and SiHa cells used in the study. The images showed a colocalization coincidence rate of 96.0 %, 95.0 %, 98.0 %, and 96.0 %, respectively as illustrated in Fig. S4. This indicates that BSN-CDs possess exceptional cell imaging capabilities with low toxicity, suggesting their potential as a platform for numerous biological applications.

To sum up, we have successfully developed a novel, straightforward, and quick fluorometric method for sensing pepsin activity using BSN-CDs for the first time. The synthesized BSN-CDs exhibit red fluorescence at 600 nm when excited at 510 nm, with uniform morphology and high dispersibility in aqueous solutions. The co-doping of B, S, and N atoms endows BSN-CDs with exceptional optical characteristics, and stable fluorescence, adding pepsin significantly enhances the FL indicator of BSN-CDs, attributed to surface state passivation via pepsin binding. This method allows pepsin activity measurement in the range of 0.0–60 µg mL−1 with high selectivity and sensitivity, achieving a detection limit of 0.24 µg mL−1. Practicality was demonstrated by determining pepsin activity in human saliva samples with successful recoveries. Furthermore, the BSN-CDs exhibit promising biocompatibility, and low cytotoxicity on HepG2 and SiHa cells., highlighting their potential in biomedicine. This innovative sensing approach provides a new framework for creating nanostructures with tailored properties and eco-friendly synthesis for enzymes activity sensing and cell imaging.

Haitham Saad Al-mashriqi: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Curating and validating data, Writing - original draft. Pascaline Sanga: Methodology. Eskandar Qaed: Methodology. Jia Chen: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Resources. Xin Li: Methodology. Yunyun Zhang: Methodology Hongdeng Qiu: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the Light of West China“ Program from the Chinese Academy of Science (xbzg-zdsys-202008), National Natural Science Foundation of China (22374159), Youth Innovation Promotion Association CAS (2021420) and ANSO Scholarship for Young Talents in China, for the Chinese Academy of Sciences, and ”Sponsored by UCAS Scholarship for International Students“.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2025.116349.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
